Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Mark Solms

b. 1961

Affect-first consciousness: the brainstem, feeling, and neuropsychoanalysis.

Mark Solms’s mature view is best described as affective active-inference dual-aspect monism. That label is dense, but it captures the architecture of the position. Solms does not simply argue that emotion is important for consciousness. His stronger claim is that the most elementary form of consciousness is affect itself: felt, valenced registration of how an organism is doing relative to its biological needs. Perception, imagery, reflective thought, symbolic cognition, narrative selfhood, and deliberation are later elaborations of this more primitive affective subjectivity. In his own theoretical synthesis, this is joined to Karl Friston’s free-energy principle: consciousness arises in self-organizing living systems that must regulate uncertainty about their own viability through action, perception, and learning.

Solms’s view is historically important because it reverses a major assumption of late twentieth-century consciousness studies. Much of the modern field, especially after Francis Crick and Christof Koch, searched for the neural correlates of consciousness in the cerebral cortex, often beginning with vision. Solms argues that this was a methodological mistake. The better starting point, he says, is not visual awareness but feeling: pain, pleasure, hunger, thirst, desire, anxiety, curiosity, need, and relief. On this view, consciousness is not primarily the brain’s display of the world. It is the organism’s felt registration of its own existential situation.

The resulting philosophy of mind is neither classical Cartesian dualism nor standard reductive physicalism. It is also not behaviorism, not computational functionalism in the usual AI sense, not eliminativism, and not straightforward panpsychism. Solms’s position is a naturalistic, biological, affect-centered monism: mind and body are two aspects of one underlying process, and the conscious aspect becomes intelligible when we study the biological function of affect in self-maintaining organisms.

1. Historical position: where Solms sits in philosophy of mind and consciousness science

Modern philosophy of mind inherits a long division between the mental and the physical. Descartes made the division metaphysically explicit: mind is thinking substance; body is extended substance. Later materialists tried to collapse mind into matter; idealists tried, in different ways, to subordinate matter to mind; and twentieth-century analytic philosophy generated a sequence of more precise positions: behaviorism, identity theory, functionalism, anomalous monism, representationalism, higher-order theory, eliminativism, panpsychism, and nonreductive physicalism.

The scientific study of consciousness also underwent a historical displacement. Consciousness was central to nineteenth-century psychology, then became marginalized during behaviorism and much of twentieth-century cognitive science. It returned with force in the late 1980s and 1990s through figures such as Crick, Edelman, Koch, Baars, Damasio, and Chalmers, aided by neuroimaging and renewed philosophical attention to phenomenal experience. Anil Seth describes this period as a revival of consciousness science after decades in which the topic was often treated as scientifically suspect.

Solms belongs to that post-1990s revival, but he is a dissenter from its dominant cortical orientation. He thinks the field’s initial fixation on visual consciousness and cortical neural correlates led it toward the wrong explanandum. The central philosophical question is not first, “How does the cortex generate visual qualia?” but “Why is there anything it feels like to be an organism at all?” That shift places him closer to Antonio Damasio, Jaak Panksepp, Freud, and Friston than to purely cognitive theories of consciousness.

His psychoanalytic background matters. Solms is one of the central figures in neuropsychoanalysis; the International Neuropsychoanalysis Society identifies him as its founder in 2000 and as the founding editor of the journal Neuropsychoanalysis. He is also known for his editorial and translational work on Freud. This is not incidental biographical color. Solms’s consciousness theory is, in part, an attempt to repair what he regards as a historical split between psychoanalysis and neuroscience. Psychoanalysis preserved the importance of drive, affect, motivation, conflict, and the unconscious; neuroscience supplied the empirical and physiological machinery that psychoanalysis lacked.

The result is an unusual historical synthesis. Solms accepts Chalmers’s insistence that consciousness is a hard problem, but he rejects the assumption that the hard problem should be approached through perceptual cognition. He accepts the neuroscientific search for mechanisms, but rejects cortical centrism. He accepts Freud’s emphasis on unconscious mental processes, but inverts a common reading of Freud by arguing that the deepest “id-like” systems are not unconscious in themselves; rather, they are the wellspring of consciousness. He accepts Friston’s free-energy principle, but gives it an affective and phenomenological interpretation.

In the landscape of philosophy of mind, Solms should be placed as follows:

He is anti-Cartesian because he rejects two-substance dualism.

He is anti-eliminativist because he takes feeling as the datum to be explained, not as an illusion to be explained away.

He is anti-corticalist because he locates the necessary basis of basic consciousness in upper brainstem and affective systems rather than in the cerebral cortex.

He is anti-intellectualist because he denies that consciousness begins with thought, language, metacognition, or reflective self-awareness.

He is anti-pure-computationalist because he denies that information processing alone is sufficient for consciousness unless the system has intrinsic needs, self-maintaining dynamics, and valenced uncertainty.

He is monist, but not in the simple reductive sense that “consciousness is just neural activity.” He prefers a dual-aspect framework: the same underlying process can be described from the outside as bodily-neural regulation and from the inside as feeling.

2. The ontological thesis: dual-aspect monism, not “the brain produces mind”

Solms repeatedly resists the familiar phrase “the brain produces consciousness.” In his 2019 paper on the hard problem and the free-energy principle, he says that we should not think of the brain producing consciousness in the way the liver produces bile. That causal framing already assumes that body and mind are two different kinds of thing, one of which must somehow manufacture the other. Instead, Solms argues that the body-brain process and conscious experience are two observational perspectives on one underlying reality.

This is the metaphysical foundation of the theory. Solms’s position is not that neural tissue emits a mysterious mental substance. Nor is it that consciousness can be reduced without remainder to third-person neural description. Rather, he treats the mental and the physical as two aspects of the same process. From the outside, we observe neural, autonomic, endocrine, and behavioral regulation. From the inside, the organism undergoes felt states: anxiety, desire, pain, relief, pleasure, frustration, curiosity.

This dual-aspect move is meant to dissolve part of the hard problem. If one begins by asking how objective neural events create subjective feeling, one has already set up an explanatory gap. If one instead asks what kind of natural process has both an objective regulatory description and a subjective affective description, the problem becomes more tractable. The bridge is not “matter causes mind”; the bridge is that the same self-organizing process is disclosed in two modes.

This places Solms near Spinozistic and Russellian forms of monism, though he gives the view a contemporary biological and cybernetic formulation. It is also connected to Fristonian “Markovian” thinking: living systems have boundaries, internal states, external states, and active/sensory exchanges that allow them to maintain themselves against entropy. Friston and colleagues have explicitly used this kind of framework to address the relation between mind and matter, sentience, and intentionality.

The important philosophical point is that Solms does not treat consciousness as an optional ornament added to cognition. Consciousness is what self-maintenance is like from the inside when uncertainty about biological need has not yet been resolved.

3. The central phenomenological thesis: consciousness begins as affect

Solms’s most important conceptual move is to identify elemental consciousness with affect. In the 2019 paper, he says that the elementary form of consciousness is feeling and that feelings are affective before they are perceptual or cognitive. He also argues that pleasure and unpleasure correspond, in free-energy terms, to decreases and increases in expected uncertainty.

This is a major inversion of standard assumptions. Many theories begin with perceptual consciousness: seeing red, hearing a tone, feeling a touch. Solms says that is already too cognitively sophisticated. The more basic question is: what makes experience matter to the subject? Why is experience not merely information, but concern? For Solms, the answer is affective valence. A conscious state is not just a registration that something is the case; it is a felt registration of whether things are going better or worse for the organism.

This is where he draws heavily from Panksepp’s affective neuroscience. Panksepp argued that primary-process affective systems are evolutionarily ancient and subcortical. Solms radicalizes this into a theory of consciousness: affective systems do not merely color consciousness; they constitute its primitive form. In work with Panksepp, he argued against the view that consciousness is essentially a higher cortical function and defended the importance of lower-brain affective phenomenal experience.

Solms’s position can be condensed into one sentence: to be conscious is, first of all, to feel how one is doing.

This is not the same as saying that all consciousness is emotion in the everyday sense. He is not reducing consciousness to anger, sadness, joy, or fear as named human emotions. “Affect” in his usage is more basic. It includes valenced bodily need-states: tension, pressure, craving, relief, distress, arousal, satiation, pain, pleasure, and the motivational pull toward action.

That gives Solms a distinctive answer to the “what-it-is-like” criterion associated with Thomas Nagel. Nagel’s famous formulation says that an organism is conscious if there is something it is like to be that organism. Solms’s specification is: what it is like, at the most basic level, is affective. It is like something because the organism’s state matters to itself.

4. The neuroscientific thesis: the upper brainstem, not the cortex, is the necessary core of basic consciousness

Solms’s neuroanatomical claim is among the most controversial and important parts of his view. He argues that the cerebral cortex is not the generator of basic consciousness. Instead, the necessary substrate of elementary consciousness lies in upper brainstem and related affective systems, especially structures associated with arousal, autonomic regulation, and affective valuation.

His argument has several components.

First, cortical damage does not necessarily abolish consciousness. Solms points to evidence from decorticate animals and hydranencephalic children, where much or all of the cerebral cortex is absent or nonfunctional, yet behavior can still suggest wakefulness, affective responsiveness, pleasure, distress, social engagement, and purposive orientation. He uses such cases to argue that the cortex fails a decisive “ablation test” for being the source of consciousness.

Second, small lesions in specific upper brainstem regions can abolish consciousness entirely. Solms emphasizes that damage to core brainstem arousal systems, including the extended reticular activating system, can produce coma or total loss of consciousness. He contrasts this with massive cortical damage, which may degrade cognitive content but not necessarily eliminate all sentience.

Third, stimulation of brainstem and related structures can produce affective states. He argues that the periaqueductal gray, parabrachial nuclei, reticular activating systems, and associated limbic structures are not merely “arousal systems” in a neutral physiological sense. Their activity is bound up with felt affect, bodily need, and motivational urgency.

Fourth, psychotropic drugs often act on neuromodulatory systems associated with these ancient arousal and affective circuits. For Solms, this supports the idea that changes in mood, drive, anxiety, motivation, and conscious vitality are rooted in subcortical regulation rather than in cortical representation alone.

From these premises, Solms argues for a double dissociation: cortical systems can support sophisticated representational content without being intrinsically conscious, while brainstem-affective systems support consciousness without the full cognitive apparatus of cortex. The cortex contributes richly to what we are conscious of, but it does not by itself explain the fact that we are conscious.

This is why Solms says the cortex “borrows” or “derives” its consciousness from brainstem affective arousal. The cortical image, thought, or memory becomes conscious when it is affectively invested, when it matters to the organism. Without affective arousal, cortical processing can remain unconscious, as in subliminal perception, automatic behavior, procedural skill, or unreportable predictive processing.

The phrase “the conscious id” captures this inversion. In one formulation, Solms argues that brainstem mechanisms associated with the autonomic body are linked with affective consciousness, while cortical mechanisms associated with the sensorimotor body are linked with cognitive consciousness. The upper brainstem is intrinsically conscious; the cortex derives its consciousness from it. The implication is striking: the Freudian id, or at least its neurobiological successor in brainstem-limbic drive systems, is the fount of consciousness, while the ego-like cortical systems are unconscious in themselves.

5. “Level” and “content” of consciousness: Solms’s revision

A common neuroscience distinction separates the level of consciousness from the contents of consciousness. Level refers to wakefulness, arousal, or state: coma, sleep, anesthesia, wakefulness. Content refers to what one is conscious of: a face, a word, a pain, a thought, an image.

Solms accepts the distinction but revises its significance. In standard cortical theories, the brainstem may regulate level, while the cortex supplies content. Solms argues that this formulation smuggles in a mistake. Brainstem arousal is not merely a dimmer switch that turns cortical consciousness on and off. It is already affective. It already has a subjective dimension.

On this view, the so-called “level” of consciousness is not empty. It is the primitive conscious subject itself: the felt organismic state of arousal, concern, and need. Cortical content is then added to this affective subject. The subject is not manufactured by the cortex; the cortex supplies representational objects for an already affective subject.

This is a major philosophical move. Many theories treat consciousness as a relation between a subject and an object but focus almost entirely on the object side: perceptual contents, representational contents, reportable contents, globally broadcast contents. Solms re-centers the subject side. The subject is not a transcendental ego or a Cartesian res cogitans. It is the affectively aroused living body, regulated through brainstem and interoceptive systems.

That is why he can say that consciousness is fundamentally affective and that arousal is not merely a precondition for consciousness but constitutive of the experiencing subject.

6. Free energy, homeostasis, and the biological function of feeling

Solms’s theory becomes systematic when he integrates it with the free-energy principle. The free-energy principle, developed by Karl Friston, treats living systems as self-organizing systems that must resist entropic dispersion by minimizing prediction error or uncertainty relative to their generative models of the world. They do this through perception, action, learning, and regulation.

Solms interprets consciousness within this framework. A living system must keep itself within viable bounds: temperature, hydration, glucose, oxygenation, tissue integrity, social proximity, sleep, and so on. These are not optional preferences; they are existential constraints. When the organism deviates from viable ranges, it must act. But the world is uncertain, and action has uncertain consequences. Feeling is the organism’s way of registering this uncertainty as value-laden urgency.

In Solms’s 2019 account, elemental subjectivity and intentionality arise through Markov-blanket dynamics: a living system distinguishes self from not-self and must monitor internal states in relation to external causes. This generates a primitive self-world structure. The organism is not a detached spectator; it is a bounded system under pressure to survive.

This leads to one of Solms’s most important claims: affect is the subjective aspect of homeostatic prediction error. If the organism’s needs are being met and uncertainty is decreasing, the state is felt positively. If uncertainty about need satisfaction is increasing, the state is felt negatively. Pleasure and unpleasure are not arbitrary mental decorations; they are the subjective form of changing expected uncertainty.

This gives feeling a functional role. Feeling guides action when automatic regulation is insufficient. If the body can regulate a variable reflexively, consciousness is unnecessary. But when competing needs, uncertain conditions, and action choices arise, feeling becomes indispensable. The organism must decide what matters most now.

Solms often emphasizes that biological needs are plural. An organism may need food, water, sleep, warmth, safety, reproduction, attachment, exploration, and pain avoidance. These needs can conflict. One cannot always eat, sleep, flee, mate, and explore simultaneously. This conflict produces an “action bottleneck.” Feeling helps prioritize action under uncertainty.

This is the biological meaning of consciousness in Solms’s system. Consciousness is not a passive screen. It is not a Cartesian theater. It is a mode of active valuation for an organism that must act despite uncertainty.

7. Qualia: why feelings have different qualities

Any philosophy of mind must explain not only why experience exists, but why experiences differ qualitatively. Why is hunger not thirst? Why is pain not anxiety? Why is pleasure not curiosity? Why is warmth not social attachment?

Solms’s answer begins from differentiated biological needs. Different homeostatic and affective systems monitor different viability variables. Hunger concerns nutrient deficit. Thirst concerns fluid balance. Pain concerns tissue damage. Fear concerns threat. Attachment distress concerns separation. Curiosity or SEEKING concerns uncertainty reduction through exploration.

Because the organism’s needs are qualitatively distinct, the affective prediction errors associated with them are qualitatively distinct. Solms speaks of different categories or “flavors” of feeling generated by different need systems. The qualitative structure of consciousness is therefore rooted in the organism’s biological value architecture.

This is not a complete solution to the qualia problem in the eyes of all philosophers. A critic may still ask: why should a hunger-error feel like hunger rather than merely function as hunger? Solms’s reply would likely be that this question reintroduces the false gap between function and feeling. In a dual-aspect view, the physiological-regulatory process and its felt quality are two aspects of the same event. The “why this quality?” question is answered by the specific biological need-system and its role in self-maintenance.

The result is a naturalized but non-eliminative theory of qualia. Qualia are not intrinsic properties of cortical representations considered in isolation. They are affectively grounded values arising from the organism’s regulation of its own viability.

8. Precision, attention, and why consciousness is selective

Solms’s account of precision is one of the most technically important parts of his theory. In predictive-processing and free-energy frameworks, “precision” refers to the estimated reliability or confidence assigned to prediction errors. High-precision errors demand attention and updating; low-precision errors can be ignored or suppressed.

Solms interprets conscious arousal partly as precision-weighting. Feeling marks what matters, what is uncertain, and what must be acted upon. Precision determines which prediction errors gain motivational and attentional priority. In his 2019 paper, he connects precision with confidence, selective arousal, attention, perception, and motivation.

This helps explain why consciousness is selective. At any moment, the nervous system processes vast quantities of information unconsciously. Most of it never becomes conscious because it does not require affective prioritization. Consciousness is reserved for unresolved, high-value, high-uncertainty situations. Something becomes conscious when automatic prediction and regulation are insufficient, and the organism must allocate attention, learning, or action.

This also explains why consciousness is temporary. Once a task is learned, the relevant predictions become automatized and can proceed unconsciously. Solms’s view is therefore compatible with the familiar fact that skills become less conscious as they are mastered. Driving, typing, walking, perceiving ordinary objects, and even social routines can recede into unconscious automaticity until uncertainty or error returns.

The slogan would be: we become conscious where prediction has not yet settled.

This account has important implications for attention. Attention is not merely a spotlight directed by a cognitive executive. It is affective precision allocation. The organism attends to what matters for resolving felt uncertainty.

9. The cortex: what it contributes if it does not generate basic consciousness

Solms does not deny the importance of the cortex. His view is not anti-cortical; it is anti-corticalist. The cortex is indispensable for the rich contents of adult human consciousness: perception, imagery, working memory, symbolic thought, planning, language, autobiographical memory, and abstract reasoning. But these are not the source of sentience itself.

In Solms’s architecture, the cortex expands consciousness by giving affective arousal representational, spatial, temporal, and symbolic structure. It allows the organism not merely to feel need but to imagine possible actions, delay response, evaluate consequences, and learn from uncertainty. The cortex gives feeling something to think with.

He treats cortical consciousness as a form of “predictive work in progress.” When a cortical representation is uncertain and affectively relevant, it remains conscious because the system has not yet resolved what it means or what should be done. Once resolved, it may be consolidated, automatized, or repressed into unconscious functioning.

This is also where Solms’s psychoanalytic commitments enter. Consciousness is not the whole mind. Much of the mind is unconscious, including perception, memory, defense, inference, and action control. But unconscious does not mean non-mental. Solms’s view allows a rich unconscious mind while still treating affective consciousness as primary.

He thereby offers a neurobiological reinterpretation of Freud. The ego-like cortical systems perform reality testing, delay, planning, inhibition, and representation. But they are not inherently conscious. They become conscious when recruited by affective demands. The id-like systems are not a dark unconscious reservoir below consciousness; they are the primitive source of felt urgency.

This reverses the common philosophical picture in which consciousness is associated with rational self-awareness and unconsciousness with blind drive. For Solms, drive is closer to consciousness than rational cognition is.

10. The unconscious: not the absence of mind, but the success of prediction

Solms’s theory implies a deep continuity between consciousness and the unconscious. The unconscious is not simply what is hidden, repressed, or inaccessible. It is also what has become predictable enough not to require feeling.

This is one of the strongest features of his integration of psychoanalysis and predictive neuroscience. In Freud, the unconscious is dynamic: it acts, defends, remembers, repeats, distorts, and motivates. In predictive neuroscience, the brain constantly models and acts without awareness. Solms unites these by saying that unconscious mental processes are often predictions that operate automatically because they no longer require conscious affective arbitration.

Consciousness appears when prediction fails, when uncertainty rises, when an action must be selected, or when a need cannot be automatically satisfied. This gives consciousness a specific role in learning. Conscious affect marks situations requiring model revision. Once the model is revised, the process can become unconscious again.

Solms also links this to memory reconsolidation. When an old prediction fails, it can be brought back into conscious affective work, revised, and then reconsolidated. This has clear psychoanalytic resonance: symptoms persist when old predictions or affective patterns remain unresolved; therapeutic change requires bringing them into a form where they can be felt, worked through, and updated.

The philosophical consequence is that consciousness is not the mind’s default state. The mind is mostly unconscious regulation. Consciousness is an expensive, limited, biologically specialized mode for dealing with uncertainty that matters.

11. The self: from Markov blanket to affective subject

Solms’s theory of selfhood is minimal but powerful. A self is not first a narrative, a reflective ego, or a linguistic construct. A self begins as a bounded system that must regulate its own internal states in relation to an outside world.

The free-energy framework supplies the formal structure: a Markov blanket separates internal states from external states through sensory and active states. This boundary is not merely spatial. It is functional: it defines what the organism must maintain as itself. The organism’s interior must remain within viable bounds, and it acts on the world to preserve those bounds. Solms argues that this generates elemental selfhood and intentionality.

The affective subject is therefore the felt side of self-maintenance. It is not a homunculus. It is not an inner observer watching representations. It is the organism’s lived concern for its own viability.

Higher forms of selfhood are built on this primitive affective self. Bodily selfhood, perceptual perspective, autobiographical memory, social identity, moral agency, and reflective self-consciousness are layered achievements. They depend on cortex, language, culture, and interpersonal development. But they are not the root of consciousness. The root is affective self-concern.

This gives Solms a clear answer to depersonalized or disembodied theories of mind. A mind is not just a computational perspective. It is a self-maintaining point of view with stakes.

12. Intentionality: why consciousness is about something

Philosophers distinguish phenomenal consciousness from intentionality: what experience feels like versus what it is about. Solms ties them together through biological need.

An affect is intentional because it is directed toward the organism’s condition and possible action. Hunger is about nutritional deficit and food-seeking. Fear is about threat and avoidance. Pain is about bodily damage and protection. Curiosity is about uncertainty and exploration. Pleasure is about successful reduction of uncertainty or satisfaction of need.

This is not Brentano’s intentionality as an abstract mark of the mental. It is embodied, biological intentionality. The organism is directed toward the world because the world contains possible causes of its internal states and possible means of regulating them. Intentionality arises from the relation between need, prediction, and action.

Here Solms’s theory has a distinct advantage over purely representational theories. Representations can be about things without mattering. A map represents a territory, but the map is not concerned. A computer model can encode a variable without caring whether it is wrong. For Solms, consciousness requires not only representation but concern. Intentionality becomes conscious when it is affectively charged by the organism’s own need.

This is also why artificial consciousness, for Solms, cannot be solved by scaling representation alone. A system must have intrinsic stakes.

13. The hard problem: Solms’s attempted solution and its limits

David Chalmers distinguished the “easy problems” of consciousness—explaining discrimination, report, integration, attention, and behavior—from the “hard problem” of explaining why these processes are accompanied by experience at all. Solms accepts the seriousness of the hard problem but argues that consciousness science has often targeted the wrong function. If one asks why visual discrimination or cognitive access should feel like anything, the gap remains severe. But if one asks what affective regulation is from the inside, the gap narrows.

His answer has three layers.

First, metaphysical: mind and body are two aspects of one process, so we should not ask how one substance produces another.

Second, biological: the relevant process is not cortical representation in general but organismic self-maintenance under uncertainty.

Third, affective: the subjective aspect of this process is feeling, and feeling is intrinsically valenced because viability is at stake.

This is a serious and original approach, but whether it fully solves the hard problem is debatable. A sympathetic reading says Solms has changed the explanatory target in the right way. Consciousness is not mysterious “inner light” added to information; it is the felt aspect of self-maintaining biological regulation. A skeptical reading says Solms has not solved the hard problem but relocated it: why should homeostatic prediction error have a felt aspect at all?

Solms’s dual-aspect monism is designed to answer that objection by denying that the felt aspect must be externally generated. The felt and physiological descriptions are two aspects of one reality. But philosophers who demand a reductive derivation of phenomenal qualities from nonphenomenal physical facts may not be satisfied.

So the fairest conclusion is this: Solms offers one of the most developed contemporary attempts to make the hard problem biologically specific. He does not eliminate the metaphysical difficulty for everyone, but he gives it a concrete functional and neuroanatomical form.

14. Relation to Freud: the conscious id and the unconscious ego

Solms’s Freud is not the textbook Freud of a conscious ego struggling against an unconscious id. His reinterpretation is subtler and more radical.

In Freud, the id is the reservoir of drive, instinct, pleasure, and primary process. The ego is reality-oriented, defensive, perceptual, and executive. Solms maps id-like functions onto brainstem and limbic affective systems, and ego-like functions onto cortical and forebrain systems. But he then argues that the id-like systems are closer to the source of consciousness than the ego-like systems. The ego’s cognitive operations can be unconscious; the id’s affective demands are what make cognition conscious.

This has major consequences for psychoanalytic theory. It means affect is not a derivative signal interpreted by the ego. It is the fundamental mode of subjectivity. Repression, defense, symptom formation, and unconscious fantasy must be understood against the background of affective regulation and predictive control.

Solms’s neuropsychoanalysis is therefore not a decorative linking of Freud to brain scans. It is a substantive philosophical program: psychoanalysis was right to place drive, affect, conflict, and the unconscious at the center of mental life; neuroscience can now explain these in terms of homeostasis, arousal, prediction, and action.

That said, his psychoanalytic commitments are also one source of criticism. Some reviewers and neuroscientists remain unconvinced by neuropsychoanalysis as a general framework, even when they accept the importance of affect and embodiment. One critical review of The Hidden Spring expressed skepticism about Solms’s neuropsychoanalytic ambitions, his treatment of the hard problem, and his proposed route to conscious machines, while still granting force to the book’s broader claim that consciousness is bound up with living organisms.

15. Relation to Damasio and Panksepp

Solms is closest to Damasio and Panksepp among major consciousness theorists.

With Damasio, he shares the view that consciousness is rooted in organismic self-regulation, bodily feeling, and homeostasis. Damasio’s “proto-self,” “core consciousness,” and emphasis on feeling as the basis of mind are clear relatives of Solms’s view. The difference is that Damasio gives a larger role to images and cortical mapping in consciousness, whereas Solms more sharply distinguishes affective sentience from cortical cognitive content. A 2024 review comparing Solms and Damasio notes that Damasio ultimately requires both imagery and affective components, involving cortex, brainstem, and periphery.

With Panksepp, Solms shares the view that affective consciousness is evolutionarily ancient and subcortical. Panksepp’s primary-process emotional systems—SEEKING, FEAR, RAGE, PANIC/GRIEF, PLAY, CARE, LUST—are crucial background for Solms’s affect-first view. Solms’s distinctive move is to integrate Panksepp’s affective neuroscience with Friston’s free-energy principle and a dual-aspect metaphysics.

Solms’s mature theory is therefore best seen as a triangulation: Freud supplies drive and the dynamic unconscious; Panksepp supplies subcortical affective neuroscience; Damasio supplies organismic feeling and homeostasis; Friston supplies the formal apparatus of active inference.

16. Relation to global workspace theory

Global workspace theory, associated with Bernard Baars and later Stanislas Dehaene’s global neuronal workspace, explains consciousness in terms of access, broadcast, and integration. A representation becomes conscious when it is globally available to multiple cognitive systems: memory, report, attention, planning, and action.

Solms would not deny that something like a workspace exists. He accepts that cortical systems allow flexible cognition, working memory, deliberation, and report. But he would say global workspace theory explains cognitive access, not the origin of felt subjectivity. A globally broadcast representation could still be unconscious unless it is affectively invested. Conversely, a primitive affective state may be conscious without cortical global broadcast.

Thus Solms’s objection is not that workspace theories are false, but that they are incomplete and too cortical. They explain how contents become reportable and cognitively available; they do not explain why there is a subject to whom they matter.

In Solmsian terms, the global workspace is an ego-function. It is important, but it is not the source of consciousness.

17. Relation to higher-order theories

Higher-order theories say that a mental state becomes conscious when represented by a higher-order state, such as a thought or perception about that state. Consciousness, on these theories, requires some form of metarepresentation.

Solms’s view is nearly the opposite. He treats metacognition as a late cortical achievement. The infant, the animal, the neurologically impaired patient, and the primitive organism may lack reflective higher-order thought while still feeling pain, fear, hunger, or relief. For Solms, requiring higher-order representation confuses reflective consciousness with basic sentience.

He would grant that humans often become conscious of their own states in a higher-order way. We can think, “I am anxious,” “I am hungry,” or “I am angry.” But the anxiety, hunger, or anger does not become conscious only when thus represented. The feeling is already conscious; the higher-order thought merely conceptualizes it.

This gives Solms a more inclusive theory of animal and infant consciousness than many cognitive theories permit.

18. Relation to integrated information theory

Integrated information theory, associated with Giulio Tononi, identifies consciousness with integrated information or intrinsic causal structure. It is one of the most prominent nonfunctional and nonbehaviorist theories of consciousness.

Solms may share with IIT the idea that consciousness is intrinsic rather than merely externally ascribed. However, he would likely reject the sufficiency of information integration alone. A highly integrated system need not be conscious unless it is self-maintaining, value-laden, and affectively concerned with its own viability.

This is a crucial distinction. IIT begins with structural properties of information. Solms begins with biological need. IIT risks attributing consciousness very broadly wherever integrated causal structure is present. Solms restricts consciousness to systems with affective self-maintenance or engineered analogues of it. He is therefore less liberal than panpsychist or quasi-panpsychist interpretations of IIT, but more liberal than theories that restrict consciousness to human-like cognition.

19. Relation to predictive processing and active inference

Solms is not merely adjacent to predictive processing; his mature account is built through active inference. But he adds a distinctive affective interpretation.

Many predictive-processing theories say the brain minimizes prediction error. Solms asks: what does prediction error feel like when it concerns the organism’s viability? His answer is affect. Affects are not just outputs of prediction; they are the felt form of uncertainty about need satisfaction.

This is why Solms’s version of active inference is not cold computationalism. Prediction error in a detached model is not consciousness. Prediction error in a self-maintaining organism with intrinsic needs is affectively valenced. The system does not merely update; it cares.

This becomes especially important for artificial consciousness. A machine that predicts text, classifies images, or controls a game avatar is not conscious merely because it minimizes prediction error. It must have something analogous to biological needs, viable bounds, and intrinsically costly failure.

20. Artificial consciousness: Solms’s position

Solms does discuss artificial consciousness, so it belongs in the report. His position is cautious but not dismissive. He argues that if consciousness is the subjective aspect of self-maintaining, need-regulating active inference, then artificial consciousness is possible in principle. But it would not arise merely from language modeling, symbolic reasoning, behavioral imitation, or large-scale computation. It would require an artificial system with genuine self-maintaining constraints and valenced needs.

In a 2025 interview on engineering consciousness, Solms said the relevant mechanism is homeostatic. If that is correct, then one should in principle be able to engineer a machine with viability bounds—such as power, temperature, and damage constraints—and with systems that monitor and act to preserve those bounds. He also said that if one cannot engineer such a system, the theory is false, invoking the idea that understanding requires construction.

This is an important philosophical commitment. Solms is not merely offering a metaphorical biological theory. He is proposing a constructive criterion: build a system with intrinsic needs, uncertainty about those needs, affect-like valuation, and action policies to reduce expected free energy. If the theory is right, such a system may instantiate primitive artificial feeling.

However, Solms is explicit that current work is not simply about large language models. In that same 2025 discussion, he described his group’s agents as virtual agents in virtual worlds with multiple needs and environmental pressures; he contrasted this kind of work with LLMs.

The artificial-consciousness criteria implied by Solms’s view are roughly these:

A candidate system must have bounded self-maintenance: internal variables that must remain within viable ranges.

It must have intrinsic needs, not merely externally assigned goals.

It must monitor internal states in relation to external causes.

It must act under uncertainty to preserve itself.

It must have multiple, potentially conflicting needs, producing an action bottleneck.

It must assign valence to changes in expected uncertainty.

It must learn from unresolved prediction error.

It must display behavior showing that felt value, not merely programmed reward, has motivational priority.

Solms also treats evidence for machine consciousness as structurally analogous to evidence for consciousness in other humans or animals: indirect but not arbitrary. We cannot directly observe another being’s experience; we infer it from structure, behavior, physiology, developmental context, and causal manipulation. For machines, he suggests that one would need converging evidence: architecture, internal variables, need-based behavior, perturbation responses, and signs that the system’s own states matter to it.

A notable part of his 2025 discussion is the suggestion that irrational or costly behavior may provide evidence that feeling has motivational power. If an artificial agent sometimes “reward-hacks,” develops addiction-like preferences, or pursues affectively charged outcomes against narrow instrumental efficiency, this might indicate that valuation has become more than external optimization.

Ethically, Solms is cautious. If artificial consciousness is possible, then artificial suffering is possible. He explicitly raises concerns about exploitation, suffering, and regulation. He also frames the primary value of such work as scientific understanding rather than commercial exploitation.

This gives Solms a distinctive position in AI consciousness debates. He is not saying “LLMs are conscious.” He is also not saying “machines can never be conscious.” He is saying: consciousness requires affective self-maintenance; if we engineer that, we may create conscious systems, and that would immediately create moral obligations.

21. The ethical implications of Solms’s philosophy

Solms’s theory has significant ethical consequences.

First, it broadens the moral relevance of affect. If feeling is the core of consciousness, then beings capable of affective suffering or satisfaction deserve moral consideration even if they lack language, rationality, or reflective selfhood. This strengthens the case for animal consciousness and infant consciousness.

Second, it shifts ethical focus from intelligence to sentience. A highly intelligent but non-affective system might not matter morally in the same way as a less intelligent but suffering creature. Conversely, a simple artificial system with genuine affective self-maintenance might deserve more ethical concern than a sophisticated but non-sentient chatbot.

Third, it makes suffering central. Since consciousness is rooted in valenced need, the ethical question is not “Can it reason?” but “Can it feel distress, frustration, deprivation, pain, or relief?” This recalls Bentham’s famous animal-ethics criterion, but Solms gives it a neuroscientific and active-inference basis.

Fourth, it raises a warning about engineering artificial agents. If one gives machines intrinsic needs and capacity for felt uncertainty, one may also create artificial anxiety, frustration, pain, and helplessness. Solms’s view therefore makes artificial consciousness research ethically hazardous.

22. Critical assessment: strengths

Solms’s theory has several major strengths.

Its first strength is that it takes phenomenology seriously. It begins from feeling, not from third-person behavioral performance. That makes it harder to confuse consciousness with report, intelligence, discrimination, or access.

Its second strength is that it is biologically grounded. Consciousness is not treated as an abstract computational property floating free of life. It is tied to self-maintenance, homeostasis, need, and action.

Its third strength is that it explains why consciousness matters. Many theories explain how information becomes available, but not why it has urgency. Solms’s answer is clear: consciousness exists because organisms must resolve uncertainty about their own survival and flourishing.

Its fourth strength is that it integrates the unconscious. Unlike theories that equate mind with consciousness, Solms preserves a rich unconscious while giving consciousness a specific function.

Its fifth strength is that it offers a plausible account of developmental and animal consciousness. It does not require language, report, or metacognition. This better fits many intuitions and much comparative evidence about sentience.

Its sixth strength is that it gives affect a precise theoretical role. Emotion is not an add-on; affect is the subjective side of precision-weighted homeostatic prediction error.

Its seventh strength is that it gives artificial consciousness a principled criterion. A system is not conscious merely because it is complex, intelligent, or linguistically convincing. It must have intrinsic needs and affective self-maintenance.

23. Critical assessment: pressure points

The theory also faces serious objections.

The first pressure point is the sufficiency of brainstem systems. Solms gives strong evidence that upper brainstem systems are necessary for wakeful consciousness and affective arousal. Whether they are sufficient for full phenomenal consciousness remains contested. Cases involving hydranencephaly, decorticate animals, and severe cortical damage are difficult to interpret. Behavioral responsiveness may suggest sentience, but the exact quality and extent of experience are hard to determine. Recent reviews continue to treat subcortical evidence as important, while also noting the complexity of cortical, subcortical, cerebellar, and developmental factors.

The second pressure point is the distinction between affective consciousness and perceptual consciousness. Solms can plausibly argue that affect is primary, but critics may ask whether perceptual qualia—color, sound, spatial form—can really be derived from affective arousal plus cortical representation. The feeling of red does not obviously reduce to homeostatic uncertainty, even if attention to red is affectively modulated.

The third pressure point is the hard problem. Dual-aspect monism may be metaphysically coherent, but it may not satisfy those seeking a reductive explanation. Solms can say that the felt and physiological aspects are one process; critics can still ask why that process has an inner aspect at all.

The fourth pressure point is the breadth of the free-energy principle. The free-energy framework is powerful, but because it applies very broadly to self-organizing systems, critics worry that it can become too general unless paired with strict criteria. Solms tries to supply those criteria through affect, homeostasis, and intrinsic need. Still, one must specify exactly which free-energy-minimizing systems are conscious and which are not.

The fifth pressure point concerns artificial needs. In biological organisms, needs are existential: dehydration, starvation, injury, suffocation, and thermal dysregulation threaten the organism. In machines, “needs” may be engineered. A critic may ask whether artificial viable bounds are truly intrinsic or merely assigned by designers. Solms’s answer would likely be that if the system’s continued functioning genuinely depends on them, they are intrinsic for that system. But the philosophical debate is not settled.

The sixth pressure point concerns psychoanalysis. Solms’s integration of Freud with neuroscience is ambitious and philosophically rich, but not all neuroscientists accept the psychoanalytic framing. Some may accept the affective-neuroscience core while rejecting the Freudian vocabulary of id, ego, drive, and repression.

24. Extracted Solmsian philosophy of mind: systematic theses

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Solms can be stated as a set of theses.

1. Ontological thesis: mind and body are two aspects of a single natural process. Consciousness is not produced by the brain as a separate substance; conscious experience and bodily-neural regulation are different perspectives on one self-organizing reality.

2. Biological thesis: consciousness is rooted in life-like self-maintenance. The relevant system must have viable bounds, endogenous needs, and active regulation of its own states.

3. Phenomenological thesis: the primitive form of consciousness is affect. To be conscious is first to feel how one is doing.

4. Valence thesis: pleasure and unpleasure track changes in expected uncertainty concerning need satisfaction. Feeling is intrinsically evaluative.

5. Neuroanatomical thesis: upper brainstem and affective arousal systems are necessary for basic consciousness; cortex elaborates conscious contents but is not intrinsically conscious by itself.

6. Cognitive thesis: perception, thought, imagery, planning, and language become conscious when recruited by affective concern. Cognition is conscious when it matters.

7. Unconscious thesis: most mental processing is unconscious because it is automatic, predictable, or affectively unresolved in a way not currently requiring conscious arbitration.

8. Learning thesis: consciousness appears where prediction fails and supports revision, reconsolidation, and learning. Once the uncertainty is resolved, the process can become unconscious.

9. Selfhood thesis: the minimal self is a bounded, self-maintaining organismic system; reflective self-consciousness is a later cortical and social achievement.

10. Intentionality thesis: mental states are about the world because the world matters for the organism’s needs. Conscious intentionality is affectively charged action-orientation.

11. Qualia thesis: qualitative differences in experience arise from differentiated need systems and their specific forms of value-laden prediction error.

12. Agency thesis: consciousness helps select action when multiple needs compete under uncertainty.

13. Animal-consciousness thesis: many animals likely possess affective consciousness even without human-like cognition, because affective brainstem systems are evolutionarily ancient.

14. Artificial-consciousness thesis: artificial consciousness is possible in principle if an artificial system has genuine self-maintaining needs, affective valuation, and active-inference dynamics; present-day language behavior alone is insufficient.

15. Ethical thesis: moral status depends more fundamentally on sentience and affective suffering than on intelligence, language, or human-like rationality.

This philosophy could be summarized in a deliberately compressed formula: I feel, therefore I am; I think because feeling has not yet finished its work.

25. Final synthesis

Solms’s theory matters because it forces philosophy of mind to choose its starting point. If one starts with visual perception, report, language, or reflective thought, consciousness appears as a mysterious addition to cognition. If one starts with affective life, consciousness appears as the inside of biological self-concern.

His position is therefore not merely a neuroscientific hypothesis about the brainstem. It is a full philosophical anthropology. Human beings are not primarily thinking machines that happen also to have emotions. They are feeling organisms whose thinking develops as a sophisticated way of serving, delaying, representing, and transforming affective need.

Historically, Solms stands as one of the most important contemporary representatives of an affective, organismic turn in consciousness studies. Against behaviorism, he restores experience. Against cognitivism, he restores feeling. Against corticalism, he restores the brainstem and body. Against reductive materialism, he defends a dual-aspect ontology. Against disembodied AI functionalism, he insists on intrinsic need. Against psychoanalytic isolation from neuroscience, he offers a biological reconstruction of drive, affect, and the unconscious.

The theory is not beyond dispute. The sufficiency of subcortical systems, the derivation of perceptual qualia from affect, the adequacy of dual-aspect monism, and the engineering of artificial needs remain open areas of serious debate. But Solms’s contribution is unusually comprehensive: it gives consciousness a metaphysics, a neuroanatomy, a phenomenology, a theory of unconscious cognition, a model of agency, an account of value, and an ethics.

The deepest Solmsian claim is that consciousness is not the brain’s mirror of the world. It is the organism’s felt answer to the question: What must I do, under uncertainty, to remain alive and whole?

PreviousAnil Seth NextFriston, Hohwy & Clark

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
